content.text: Hierarchy Design

Designing the management group hierarchy is a governance decision that shapes every policy, role assignment, and subscription you add to Azure. A well-designed hierarchy lets you assign a policy once and have it apply to dozens of subscriptions through inheritance. A poorly designed one forces you to repeat the same assignment on every subscription individually — and makes exceptions hard to reason about.

attrs.alt: Flat (anti-pattern) vs structured (recommended) management group design

content.text: The Central Design Question: What Governs Together?

The hierarchy should reflect governance boundaries, not organisational charts or team names. Two subscriptions should be siblings in the same management group if they share the same policy set — the same allowed regions, the same compliance requirements, the same network topology constraints. If they have different governance needs, they belong in different groups.

Align the hierarchy to policy differences, not to team ownership. A Finance team subscription and an Engineering team subscription that both need the same PCI-DSS controls belong in the same management group — even though they are owned by different teams.
Same regulatory requirement (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001) → same management group
Same environment tier (all production subscriptions, all non-production subscriptions) → consider sibling groups under the same parent
Different network topology (hub-spoke corporate vs. internet-facing DMZ) → different management groups
Experimentation with no compliance requirement → dedicated Sandbox group isolated from production rules
The CAF Landing Zone Structure

The Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) Landing Zone hierarchy is the Microsoft-recommended starting point. It organises management groups around the three most common governance boundaries: shared platform infrastructure, regulated corporate workloads, and public-facing online services.
Management Group
Purpose
Typical policy posture
Root
Tenant-wide universal rules only
Require tags, mandatory diagnostic settings, deny classic resources
Platform
Shared infrastructure subscriptions
Tighter controls; hub network, identity, log analytics workspaces
Platform/Connectivity
Hub networking subscriptions
Deny BGP route overrides, enforce VPN/ExpressRoute standards
Platform/Identity
Active Directory / Entra Connect subscriptions
Deny internet inbound, enforce backup policies
Platform/Management
Log Analytics, Security Center subscriptions
Read-only network; deny data exfiltration paths
Corp
Regulated business workloads
Allowed regions, deny public IPs, enforce private endpoints
Corp/Production
Live production subscriptions
Strictest; require change management tags, no public storage
Corp/Non-Production
Dev/Test subscriptions under Corp
Slightly relaxed SKU restrictions; same region and compliance rules
Online
Internet-facing workloads (public websites)
Allow public IPs for load balancers; deny direct VM public IP
Sandbox
Developer experimentation
Minimal restrictions; no corporate network peering; spend cap policy
Decommissioned
Subscriptions being retired
Deny all new resource creation; read-only access
Choosing Hierarchy Depth

The maximum depth is six levels below root. In practice, three to four levels is optimal for most organisations. Each additional level adds cognitive overhead for operators trying to understand which policies apply to a subscription.
Two-level design (small organisations, 5–15 subscriptions)

Root
├── Platform
│   ├── [connectivity-sub]
│   └── [management-sub]
├── Corp
│   ├── [finance-prod-sub]
│   └── [eng-prod-sub]
└── Sandbox
    └── [dev-sandbox-sub]
Three-level design (medium organisations, 15–50 subscriptions)

Root
├── Platform
│   ├── Connectivity
│   │   └── [hub-network-sub]
│   ├── Identity
│   │   └── [identity-sub]
│   └── Management
│       └── [mgmt-sub]
├── Corp
│   ├── Production
│   │   ├── [finance-prod-sub]
│   │   └── [eng-prod-sub]
│   └── Non-Production
│       ├── [finance-dev-sub]
│       └── [eng-dev-sub]
├── Online
│   └── [public-web-sub]
└── Sandbox
    └── [sandbox-sub]
Four-level design (large organisations, 50+ subscriptions)

Root
├── Platform
│   ├── Connectivity / Identity / Management (as above)
├── Corp
│   ├── Finance
│   │   ├── Finance-Production
│   │   │   └── [finance-prod-sub]
│   │   └── Finance-NonProd
│   │       └── [finance-dev-sub]
│   └── Engineering
│       ├── Eng-Production
│       └── Eng-NonProd
├── Online
└── Sandbox

Start with the three-level design. Add a fourth level only when a business unit has genuinely different compliance requirements from other Corp workloads — not just for organisational tidiness. Deeper than four levels is almost always unnecessary and makes inheritance difficult to reason about.
Design Principles
1. Fewer assignments is better

Every policy and RBAC assignment you make at a management group scope propagates automatically to all child subscriptions. Assign at the highest level where the rule universally applies. If you assign the same policy to 20 individual subscriptions instead of once at their common parent, you have 20 assignments to audit, update, and remove.
2. Exemptions should be rare

If most subscriptions in a management group need an exemption from a policy, the policy is at the wrong level. Move it to a narrower scope where it applies universally. Exemptions create audit complexity and are easy to forget.
3. Sandbox must be isolated

Developer sandboxes need fewer restrictions to enable experimentation — different allowed regions, no private endpoint requirement, higher VM SKU flexibility. Place sandbox subscriptions in a Sandbox management group that is a sibling of Corp, not nested under it. This prevents sandbox exemptions from interfering with Corp policy.
4. Never use team names as management group names

Teams reorganise. Management group IDs are immutable. If you name a group `mg-platform-team` and the team is renamed to `infrastructure`, you end up with a misleadingly named permanent group. Use purpose-based names: `mg-connectivity`, `mg-prod`, `mg-corp`.
5. Design for future subscriptions

Every new subscription you add to the tenant will inherit from whatever management group it is placed in. Design the hierarchy so that a new subscription placed in `Corp/Production` automatically gets the correct security and compliance posture without any manual policy assignment.
Environment Segmentation: Prod/NonProd Split

The decision of whether to split production and non-production subscriptions into separate management groups — or keep them in the same group with per-subscription policy assignments — has significant operational implications.
Approach
Pros
Cons
Corp/Production and Corp/Non-Production groups
Assign stricter policies once at Prod; Dev can have relaxed SKU limits
Slightly deeper hierarchy; moving a subscription from Dev to Prod is a management action
Single Corp group, prod/nonprod via tags
Simpler hierarchy
Policy cannot be scoped to prod subscriptions without per-assignment logic; Azure Policy cannot filter by subscription tag in conditions
Separate subscriptions per workload (one sub per app)
Fine-grained cost tracking; clear blast radius
More subscriptions to manage; subscription limit (1000) can become a constraint at scale

The Prod/NonProd split is generally the right call for organisations with meaningful compliance differences between environments. A stricter allowed-regions policy for production (e.g., only `eastus`) with a more permissive one for non-production (add `australiaeast` for a regional team's development) cannot be expressed with a single management group.
Designing for Policy Inheritance: A Worked Example
The following example shows how a three-level hierarchy reduces the number of policy assignments needed for a company with 10 subscriptions across Finance, Engineering, and Sandbox.

Without hierarchy (all subscriptions flat under root):
  - 'Require environment tag'        → 10 assignments
  - 'Allowed regions: eastus,westeurope' → 10 assignments (with sandbox exempt: 9 + 1 exception)
  - 'Deny public blob access'         → 8 assignments (finance and eng prod/dev only)
  - 'Reader role for security team'  → 10 assignments
  Total: ~38 assignments to manage

With Corp / Online / Sandbox / Platform hierarchy:
  - 'Require environment tag'        → 1 assignment at Root (all 10 inherit)
  - 'Allowed regions: eastus,westeurope' → 1 at Corp+Online (8 subs inherit); Sandbox exempt
  - 'Deny public blob access'         → 1 assignment at Corp (8 subs inherit)
  - 'Reader role for security team'  → 1 at Root
  Total: ~4 assignments to manage — and exemptions are structural, not per-subscription
Migrating an Existing Flat Hierarchy

Organisations that started without a hierarchy often have all subscriptions directly under the Root Management Group. Migrating to a structured hierarchy requires care: moving a subscription to a new management group changes its inherited policies immediately. Test by moving a non-production subscription first.
Design the target hierarchy on paper and validate with policy owners
Create all target management groups (they can exist empty)
Assign target policies and RBAC to the new groups before moving any subscriptions
Move a sandbox or dev subscription first; verify its compliance state and access patterns after the move
Move production subscriptions one at a time during a maintenance window; monitor policy compliance after each move
Remove per-subscription policy assignments that are now redundant (replaced by inheritance)

# Move a subscription to a new management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name 'mg-corp-prod' \
  --subscription $PROD_SUB_ID

# Verify compliance state after move (wait ~5 min for policy evaluation)
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $PROD_SUB_ID \
  --query '{Compliant:results.policyStates.compliantCount, NonCompliant:results.policyStates.noncompliantCount}'

# List any non-compliant resources
az policy state list \
  --subscription $PROD_SUB_ID \
  --filter 'IsCompliant eq false' \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId, Policy:policyDefinitionName}' \
  -o table
Terraform Module for a Complete Landing Zone Hierarchy

The following Terraform module creates the full CAF landing zone management group structure with configurable top-level groups and Corp sub-groups. It assigns a minimal set of root-level policies and demonstrates the pattern for attaching subscriptions.

The Terraform module above provisions the entire CAF hierarchy plus baseline policies in a single `terraform apply`. Running it idempotently on each CI push ensures drift from ad-hoc portal changes is detected and reversed.
Hierarchy Design for Multi-Tenant Scenarios

Some organisations operate multiple Azure tenants — for example, a production tenant and a development tenant, or separate tenants for different subsidiaries. Each tenant has its own independent management group tree. Governance consistency across tenants requires a shared policy-as-code approach where the same Terraform modules are deployed to each tenant.
Documenting Your Hierarchy

The management group hierarchy is a critical piece of organisational infrastructure. Document it with a hierarchy diagram, the governance rationale for each group, and the policy and RBAC assignments at each level. Store this in the same repository as the Terraform code so changes are tracked together.
Generating a current hierarchy report

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Generate a markdown report of the management group hierarchy and its policy assignments

TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)

echo '# Management Group Hierarchy Report'
echo ''
echo '## Groups'
az account management-group list \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Display:displayName, Parent:details.parent.id}' \
  -o json | jq -r '.[] | "- **\(.Display)** (`\(.Name)`) → parent: \(.Parent)"'

echo ''
echo '## Policy Assignments at Root'
az policy assignment list \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TENANT_ID" \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName, Policy:policyDefinitionId}' -o table

echo ''
echo '## Policy Assignments at Corp'
az policy assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName, Policy:policyDefinitionId}' -o table
Common Hierarchy Design Mistakes
Mistake
Symptom
Fix
All subscriptions directly under Root
Policy must be assigned per-subscription; Sandbox exemptions affect Root-level assignments
Create Corp, Online, Sandbox top-level groups; move subscriptions
Using geography as the top-level grouping (US / EU / APAC)
Policy differences between regions require deep nesting; prod and nonprod in same group per region
Use compliance/environment as primary dimension; region is usually a policy parameter, not a hierarchy level
Naming groups after team names (FinanceTeam, EngTeam)
Team names change; MG IDs are immutable
Use purpose-based names: Corp, Corp-Production, Platform-Connectivity
Mixing Sandbox workloads under Corp
Sandbox relaxations (different regions, higher VM SKUs) affect Corp compliance posture
Sandbox must be a sibling of Corp, not nested under it
Creating a management group per application
Hundreds of groups; 10,000 limit becomes a concern; policy must be repeated
Group by governance tier, not by application. One subscription per application is fine; the MG is for grouping multiple subscriptions with shared governance
Not testing policy impact before moving subscriptions
Production resources suddenly non-compliant after MG move; deny effects block new deployments
Assign policies to the empty target MG, evaluate impact in audit mode, then move subscriptions
Summary

Management group hierarchy design is a one-time structural decision with long-lasting consequences. The guiding principle is to align groups with governance differences — policies that must differ between groups of subscriptions. The CAF Landing Zone structure (Root → Platform / Corp / Online / Sandbox) is the proven starting point for most organisations. Keep the hierarchy to three or four levels; design so that placing a new subscription in the correct group automatically gives it the right policy posture; and manage the entire structure in IaC from day one to prevent drift.
